subject: Probability: Definitions and Properties
topic: §7 Probability of Intersection, Union, and Complementary Event
question: If a person visits his primary care physician, suppose that the probability that he will have blood test work is 0.44, the probability that he will have an X-ray is 0.24, the probability that he will have an MRI is 0.21, the probability that he will have blood test and an X-ray is 0.08, the probability that he will have blood test and an MRI is 0.11, the probability that he will have an X-ray and an MRI is 0.07, and the probability that he will have blood test, an X-ray, and an MRI is 0.03. What is the probability that a person visiting his PCP will have at least one of these things done to him/her?
explanation: Let $B$ be the event that a person will have blood test, $X$ is the event that a person will have an X-ray, and $M$ is the event a person will have an MRI. We are given $\Pr(B) = 0.44$, $\Pr(X) = 0.24$, $\Pr(M) = 0.21$, $\Pr(B \cap X) = 0.08$, $\Pr(B \cap M) = 0.11$, $\Pr(X \cap M) = 0.07$ and $\Pr(B \cap X \cap M) = 0.03$. Thus, $\Pr(B \cup X \cup M) = 0.44 + 0.24 + 0.21 - 0.08 - 0.11 - 0.07 + 0.03 = 0.66$.
